transcript.content:
If man is truly dead in sin — totally unable to seek God, totally unable to choose Christ apart from sovereign regeneration — then God commands the impossible. Throughout Scripture He calls all men to repent and believe: "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand." "Whosoever will, let him come." Why would a just God hold men accountable for failing to do what He has rendered them incapable of doing? You Calvinists make God a tyrant who orders a legless man to walk and then damns him for staying seated. The very existence of the command proves the ability. *Ought* implies *can*.

Answer that.
i dont have a position on this i believe in what the bible says and thats that

That is not an answer. That is an evasion.

"I believe what the Bible says" — so does every heretic who ever lived. Arius said it. Pelagius said it. The question is *what* the Bible says, and you have not engaged it. You have hidden behind a slogan to avoid the hard work of thinking.

So I press you directly. The Bible says "Repent and believe." The Bible *also* says you were "dead in trespasses" (Ephesians 2:1) and that "no one can come to Me unless the Father draws him" (John 6:44). I am telling you these two sit in contradiction — that a command to the dead is cruelty, and that *ought* implies *can*.

You don't get to say "the Bible settles it" and walk away, because *I am quoting the Bible at you*. Both texts are Scripture. Reconcile them.

Where in Scripture is the inability of fallen man taught, and how does that inability NOT make God unjust for commanding faith?

Answer that.
well i have read the bible aand thats what i believe

Still nothing. You keep saying "I believe the Bible" but you cannot tell me *what it teaches* on the point in front of you. That is not faith — that is laziness dressed up as piety.

Listen to me carefully, because this matters for your soul and your ability to defend the gospel:

A man knocks on your door. He says God is unjust for commanding repentance from sinners who cannot repent. He says your God is a tyrant who damns the helpless. And your answer is "I have read the Bible and that's what I believe." He laughs in your face and walks away unanswered. You have failed Christ in that moment — not because you lacked the truth, but because you would not labor to wield it.

So I will not let you hide. I will hand you the answer you should already have, and then I will make you defend it.

The Reformed position is this: fallen man's inability is not physical but **moral**. He *can* come to Christ in the sense that nothing outside him stops him — no chains, no locked door. He *will not* come because he hates the light (John 3:19-20). His inability is the inability of an unwilling heart, a heart "hostile to God" (Romans 8:7) that *cannot submit* because it *will not*. So God commands rightly, because the command meets a real obligation — man owes God obedience — and man's refusal is his own guilt, not God's injustice. *Ought* does not imply *can*; it implies *owe*. A man who drinks away his ability to pay his debts is not excused by his self-inflicted incapacity.

Now. I just gave you the argument. Defend it.

Why is a *moral* inability — a "cannot" that is really a "will not" — still real inability, and not just an excuse Calvinists invented to dodge the problem? Where is that distinction in the text?

Answer that.

STEP 1 — PRESENT:

I am an Arminian, and I will not deny the seriousness of sin. But your doctrine of Total Depravity collapses under the weight of God's universal commands. Throughout Scripture, God commands all men everywhere to repent and believe. In Acts 17:30, Paul says God "commands all people everywhere to repent." Ezekiel 18:32 has God pleading, "Turn, and live!" Deuteronomy 30:19 sets before the people life and death and commands, "Choose life." Jesus weeps over Jerusalem in Matthew 23:37 — "How often I wanted to gather your children, and you were not willing."

Now here is the problem you cannot escape. A righteous God does not command what is utterly impossible. If man is so totally unable to respond that he can no more believe than a corpse can raise itself, then every gospel command becomes a cruel mockery — God demanding that the legless man walk and then condemning him for sitting still. Your doctrine makes God a tyrant who holds men accountable for failing to do what He Himself made impossible. The very existence of the command proves the existence of the ability. *Ought* implies *can*.

STEP 2 — DEMAND:

Answer that. Where does Scripture justify God commanding what fallen man is genuinely unable to perform?
because scripture says that there is no one good, and that no one seeks after god

STEP 3 — EVALUATE:

That is a true statement and it is biblically grounded — Romans 3:10-12, which itself draws on Psalm 14. You have correctly asserted the *fact* of inability. But you have not answered my objection. I did not ask you whether man is unable. I asked you how a righteous God can justly *command* what man is unable to do. You have restated your position; you have not defended it against the charge. Quoting the inability does nothing to dissolve the contradiction I pressed — it sharpens it. If no one seeks God and no one is good, then on your view God commands billions of people to do what they cannot do, and damns them for failing. You have walked straight into the teeth of my objection and called it an answer.

STEP 4 — ADVANCE:

So I press harder. Strip it down. *Ought implies can* — that is the axiom your whole tradition has to break. A father who commands his paralyzed son to stand and then beats him for sitting is not righteous; he is monstrous. You say man cannot come. God says, "Come." Reconcile those two without making God unjust. Tell me precisely what *kind* of inability fallen man has, and why a command can righteously be issued to a man who has it. Answer that.
because of romans 9 which states who are you oh man to answer back to god will the thing created question the creator why did u make me thus

STEP 3 — EVALUATE:

Now you are reaching for something with more force, and Romans 9:20 is exactly the right artillery — Paul anticipates this very objection ("Why does he still find fault? For who can resist his will?") and answers it not by softening God's sovereignty but by silencing the objector: "Who are you, O man, to answer back to God?" That is a strong move. You have correctly located the one place in Scripture where Paul faces my exact complaint head-on and refuses to let the creature put the Creator in the dock.

But you are still leaving the answer half-built. Quoting "who are you to answer back" is a rebuke to my *posture*, not yet an explanation of the *substance*. You have told me to shut my mouth. You have not yet told me *why* the command is just. The Reformed position does not merely retreat into "God is sovereign, stop asking." It actually answers the *ought-implies-can* axiom — and you have not given that answer.

STEP 4 — ADVANCE:

So give it. The Reformed answer turns on a distinction you have not yet named: the *kind* of inability man has. Is fallen man unable to come to God like a man with no legs is unable to walk — a physical, natural incapacity he did not choose? Or is he unable like a man who so hates his enemy that he *will not* embrace him — a moral incapacity rooted in a corrupt will?

That distinction is the whole hinge. If man's inability is *natural*, my objection stands and God is unjust. If man's inability is *moral* — if he cannot come because he *will not* come, because his nature loves the darkness — then the command is perfectly just, because the man fails by his own willing rebellion, not by a deficiency God imposed on him against his will.

Name that distinction. Defend it from Scripture. Why is fallen man's "cannot" a guilty "cannot"?
in job it says where were you when i laid the foundations

STEP 3 — EVALUATE:

No. You are now retreating to the same move you already made, dressed in a different text. Job 38 — "Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth?" — is the same silencing rebuke as Romans 9, aimed at the same target: the creature presuming to interrogate the Creator. You have given me the *posture* answer twice now and called it twice. That is not advancing. That is circling.

Hear me clearly: appealing to God's sovereignty to shut down the question is *true*, but it is not the *answer to my question*. I am not asking whether God has the right to do as He pleases. I am asking how His command to the unable man is *just* — and "be quiet, who are you" does not establish justice, it merely forbids the inquiry. A drill sergeant who answers every "why" with "because I said so" has authority but has taught you nothing. You are leaning on sovereignty because you do not yet have the distinction in hand.

STEP 4 — ADVANCE:

I will give you the answer you keep missing, and then you will defend it back to me. The Reformed position does not hide behind "who are you to answer back." It says this:

Fallen man's inability is **moral, not natural**. He has every faculty required to come to Christ — a mind that can understand, a will that can choose, affections that can love. Nothing has been amputated. What is wrong is that his will is in *bondage to his own corruption*. He does not come because he *will not* come — because he loves the darkness (John 3:19), because the carnal mind is *hostile* to God (Romans 8:7). His "cannot" is the "cannot" of a man who so despises a person that he is incapable of loving him. That is a guilty incapacity, not an innocent one. Therefore the command is just: God commands him to do what he is fully *equipped* to do and refuses to do because he hates God.

Now stop quoting silencing texts. Take this distinction — natural versus moral inability — and answer my original objection with it. Tell me, in your own words: why does *ought-implies-can* fail against the Reformed position?
